title: <div>  •  Every Fletcher-and-Andrew scene <b>ends in a completely different emot
content:
• Every Fletcher-and-Andrew scene ends in a completely different emotional state than it began:
• Starts: Fletcher praises Andrew → Ends: Fletcher throws a chair at him.
• Starts: Andrew is confident in his solo → Ends: He is sabotaged and humiliated.

McKee demands progression—every scene must force a change.

McCloud: Reader-Controlled Pacing Can Override Escalation

McCloud argues that the way a reader moves through panels controls escalation just as much as narrative design.

“In comics, time is not fixed—the reader dictates how fast or slow events unfold.”

🔥 Example from Maus
• Art Spiegelman uses panel density to manipulate pacing:
• Heavy dialogue & small panels create a claustrophobic effect, slowing time.
• Sparse pages with large images create a breath of space, making the reader pause and reflect.

📌 Key Takeaway for Graphic Novels:
context: From document: 1.1.3. | Narrative Transcends Medium | Subheading — chunk 5 (chunk 5/7)
